skills.skill: Three-dimensional shapes in the real world I
skills.prompt: This topic covers Three-dimensional shapes in the real world I for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Students identify three-dimensional shapes (cubes, rectangular prisms, spheres, cones, cylinders, pyramids) and describe their attributes. Key concepts include faces, edges, vertices, and relating 3D shapes to real objects and their 2D faces. Vocabulary: three-dimensional, solid, face, edge, vertex, cube, rectangular prism, sphere, cone, cylinder, pyramid. Common misconceptions include difficulty counting hidden attributes and confusing 2D/3D terminology. Activities should include examining real objects, building with blocks, and sorting by attributes appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.

skills.skill: Three-dimensional shapes in the real world II
skills.prompt: This topic covers Three-dimensional shapes in the real world II for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Students identify three-dimensional shapes (cubes, rectangular prisms, spheres, cones, cylinders, pyramids) and describe their attributes. Common misconceptions include difficulty counting hidden attributes and confusing 2D/3D terminology. Activities should include examining real objects, building with blocks, and sorting by attributes appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Attributes of polygons for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Polygons are closed 2D shapes with straight sides. Students classify by side count: triangle (3), quadrilateral (4), pentagon (5), hexagon (6), etc. Key concepts include polygon vs. non-polygon criteria and regular vs. irregular types. Vocabulary: polygon, closed, straight sides, triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, regular, irregular. Common misconceptions include thinking circles are polygons and not recognizing irregular forms. Activities should include sorting, naming by counting sides, drawing, and finding polygons in the environment appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Identify polygons: up to 12 sides for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Polygons are closed 2D shapes with straight sides. Students classify by side count: triangle (3), quadrilateral (4), pentagon (5), hexagon (6), etc. Key concepts include polygon vs. non-polygon criteria and regular vs. irregular types. Vocabulary: polygon, closed, straight sides, triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, hexagon, regular, irregular. Common misconceptions include thinking circles are polygons and not recognizing irregular forms. Activities should include sorting, naming by counting sides, drawing, and finding polygons in the environment appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.

skills.skill: Count and compare sides and vertices
skills.prompt: This topic covers Count and compare sides and vertices for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Students identify and count faces (flat surfaces), edges (where faces meet), and vertices (corner points) of 3D shapes. Key concepts include systematic counting, understanding that different shapes have different counts, and comparing shapes by these attributes. Vocabulary: face, edge, vertex/vertices, flat surface, corner, three-dimensional. Common misconceptions include miscounting hidden features and confusing edges with sides. Activities should include handling physical objects, marking attributes, and creating comparison charts appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Acute, obtuse, and right triangles for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(A)). Students classify triangles by angles (acute, right, obtuse) and sides (equilateral, isosceles, scalene). Every triangle belongs to one angle category and one side category. Vocabulary: acute, obtuse, right, equilateral, isosceles, scalene, angle, side, classify. Common misconceptions include confusing classifications. Activities should include measuring, sorting, and creating examples of each type appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Parallel sides in quadrilaterals for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(B)). Students classify quadrilaterals by their attributes. The hierarchy: squares are rectangles, rectangles are parallelograms, all are quadrilaterals. Trapezoids have exactly one pair of parallel sides. Vocabulary: quadrilateral, rectangle, square, rhombus, parallelogram, trapezoid, parallel, right angle. Common misconceptions include thinking categories are mutually exclusive. Activities should include sorting, Venn diagrams, and attribute-based identification games appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Identify rhombuses for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(B)). A rhombus is a quadrilateral with four equal sides. It is a special parallelogram. A square is a special rhombus with right angles. Vocabulary: rhombus, quadrilateral, parallelogram, equal sides, parallel, diagonal, angle. Common misconceptions include thinking rhombuses must be tilted and not recognizing squares as rhombuses. Activities should include sorting quadrilaterals, measuring sides, and drawing in various orientations appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

skills.skill: Find the area of rectangles and squares
skills.prompt: This topic covers Find the area of rectangles and squares for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.6(C)). Area measures the surface a shape covers in square units. Key concepts include tiling with unit squares, using Area = length x width for rectangles, and understanding square units. Vocabulary: area, square unit, cover, tile, length, width, multiply. Common misconceptions include confusing area with perimeter and not using square units. Activities should include tiling, grid counting, and formula application appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

skills.skill: Fractions of number lines: halves, fourths, and eighths
skills.prompt: This topic covers Fractions of number lines: halves, fourths, and eighths for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.7(A)). Students partition shapes into equal parts and name the fractions. Key concepts include truly equal parts, naming (halves=2 parts, fourths=4, eighths=8), and understanding that more parts means smaller pieces. Vocabulary: equal parts, partition, halves, fourths, quarters, eighths, whole, fraction. Common misconceptions include accepting unequal parts. Activities should include folding paper, cutting shapes, shading parts, and sharing objects equally appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

skills.skill: Graph unit fractions on number lines
skills.prompt: This topic covers Graph unit fractions on number lines for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.7(A)). Students place fractions on number lines by dividing intervals into equal parts. Key concepts include equal spacing, counting parts from 0, and fractions greater than 1. Vocabulary: number line, fraction, interval, equal parts, unit fraction, whole. Common misconceptions include unequal intervals and counting marks instead of spaces. Activities should include drawing, placing fraction cards, and comparing on parallel lines appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

skills.skill: Which customary unit of weight is appropriate?
skills.prompt: This topic covers Which customary unit of weight is appropriate? for Grade 3 students (TEKS 3.7(D)). Students select appropriate measurement units for different situations. Key concepts include understanding relative unit sizes, choosing sensible units (inches for small objects, feet/yards for larger), and understanding both customary and metric systems. Vocabulary: unit, inch, foot, yard, centimeter, meter, customary, metric, appropriate. Common misconceptions include confusing systems and not understanding relative sizes. Activities should include estimating, selecting tools, and discussing practical unit choices appropriate for ages 8-9. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 8-9-year-olds encounter daily.
